name: Polish Scrambled Eggs with Kielbasa
steps: Brown the sliced kielbasa in the butter until lightly crisp.
Pour in the beaten eggs and scramble gently until just set.
Serve hot with bread.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion in half the butter until soft, then add the sliced kielbasa and brown it lightly, 4-5 minutes.
Beat the eggs with the black pepper and pour into the pan, stirring gently over low heat until just set and creamy.
Serve hot with rye bread - a hearty, everyday Polish breakfast.
description: A simple egg and kielbasa breakfast.

name: Polish Placki Ziemniaczane
steps: Squeeze excess liquid from the grated potato, then mix with the egg and flour.
Fry spoonfuls flattened into pancakes, 3-4 minutes per side, until golden and crisp.
Serve warm, traditionally with sour cream.
steps_detailed: Grate the potato and onion together, then squeeze out as much excess liquid as possible using a clean towel.
Mix the grated potato and onion with the egg, flour, and a good pinch of salt into a thick batter.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry spoonfuls of the batter, flattened into thin pancakes, 3-4 minutes per side until golden brown and crisp at the edges.
Drain briefly and serve warm with a dollop of sour cream - a beloved Polish comfort food.
description: Placki ziemniaczane, fried potato pancakes, a beloved Polish comfort food.

name: Polish Farmer's Cheese Toast
steps: Toast the bread.
Spread with the cottage cheese and top with the chopped dill.
Season with a little salt.
steps_detailed: Toast the rye bread until crisp.
Spread generously with the cottage cheese (a reasonable stand-in for Polish farmer's cheese, twaróg).
Top with the chopped dill, diced tomatoes, and black pepper.
A simple, fresh, everyday Polish breakfast.
description: A simple farmer's cheese toast, twaróg being a Polish pantry staple.

name: Polish Pierogi Ruskie
steps: Make a simple dough from the flour, a pinch of salt, and warm water.
Mix the mashed potato with the cottage cheese for the filling.
Roll the dough thin, cut into rounds, fill, fold, and boil for 4-5 minutes until they float, then finish in the butter.
steps_detailed: Make a smooth dough from the flour, egg, a pinch of salt, and warm water, kneading until elastic. Rest 20 minutes.
Saute half the onion until soft and mix with the mashed potato and cottage cheese for the filling.
Roll the dough thin and cut into rounds. Place a spoonful of filling on each, fold over, and pinch the edges firmly to seal.
Boil the pierogi in batches for 4-5 minutes until they float to the surface, then drain.
Fry the boiled pierogi in the butter with the remaining onion until golden on both sides - Poland's most iconic dish.
description: Pierogi ruskie, dumplings filled with potato and cheese, are Poland's most iconic dish, found at nearly every Polish table.

name: Polish Zurek
steps: Brown the kielbasa, then add the potato and broth and simmer 15-18 minutes until tender.
Season with a splash of vinegar for the traditional tang.
Top with the sliced hard-boiled egg before serving.
steps_detailed: Brown the sliced kielbasa with the garlic for 4-5 minutes.
Add the potato and chicken broth, and simmer 18-20 minutes until the potato is tender.
Stir in the vinegar for the distinctive sour tang that gives zurek its name.
Ladle into bowls, top with the sliced hard-boiled egg and a swirl of sour cream - a beloved sour rye soup, often served at Easter.
description: Żurek, a sour rye soup often served with sausage and egg, is one of Poland's most distinctive soups.

name: Polish Golabki
steps: Blanch the cabbage leaves briefly until pliable.
Mix the beef with the cooked rice and season well.
Roll the filling in the cabbage leaves, place in a dish with the tomatoes, and bake at 350F for 45 minutes.
steps_detailed: Blanch the whole cabbage leaves in boiling water for 2-3 minutes until pliable, then drain and trim the thick center rib.
Saute the onion and garlic, then mix with the ground beef, cooked rice, and oregano, seasoning well.
Place a portion of filling in each cabbage leaf and roll up tightly, tucking in the sides.
Arrange the rolls seam-side down in a baking dish, pour the diced tomatoes and beef broth over the top.
Cover and bake at 350F (175C) for 45-50 minutes until the cabbage is tender and the filling is cooked through
description: Gołąbki, meaning little pigeons, are fresh cabbage leaves stuffed with meat and rice - a beloved Polish comfort food, genuinely distinct from Balkan sarma, which uses fermented cabbage instead.

name: Polish Rosol
steps: Simmer the chicken with the onion and carrot in 3 cups water for 30-35 minutes until the chicken is very tender.
Remove the chicken, shred it, and return it to the pot along with the noodles.
Simmer 8-10 more minutes until the noodles are tender, then season and serve.
steps_detailed: Combine the chicken, onion, carrot, garlic, and bay leaf with 3.5 cups water in a pot. Bring to a simmer.
Cook 30-35 minutes until the chicken is very tender, skimming any foam that rises to the surface.
Remove the chicken, shred it, and return it to the pot along with the egg noodles.
Simmer 8-10 more minutes until the noodles are tender.
Remove the bay leaf, stir in the dill, and serve hot - Poland's classic Sunday chicken noodle soup, often the first course of a holiday meal.
description: Rosół, a clear chicken broth soup with noodles, a Polish Sunday-dinner staple.

name: Polish Bigos
steps: Brown the pork and kielbasa together.
Add the sauerkraut and cabbage with a splash of water, and simmer, covered, 40-45 minutes until everything is tender and well combined.
Serve hot with rye bread.
steps_detailed: Brown the pork and kielbasa with the onion for 8-10 minutes.
Add the sauerkraut, cabbage, diced tomatoes, bay leaf, and allspice, along with 1/2 cup water.
Cover and simmer over low heat 45-55 minutes, stirring occasionally, until everything is tender and the flavors have melded into a rich, tangy stew.
description: Remove the bay leaf before serving - bigos, Poland's national hunter's stew, is traditionally made in large batches and tastes even better reheated the next day.

name: Polish Kotlet Schabowy
steps: Bread the pork (flour, then egg) and fry until golden and cooked through, about 4-5 minutes per side.
Serve with boiled potato and cabbage.
steps_detailed: Pound the pork to an even 1/4-inch thickness and season with salt and black pepper.
Dredge in flour, then egg, then the rye flour (standing in for fine breadcrumbs), pressing gently to help it adhere.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium-high heat and fry the pork 4-5 minutes per side until deeply golden and cooked through.
Serve with boiled potatoes and cooked cabbage - Poland's beloved breaded pork cutlet, a Sunday dinner staple.
description: Kotlet schabowy, a breaded pork cutlet, is Poland's most common everyday dinner.

name: Polish Zrazy

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Season the beef and roll each slice around a little of the chopped onion, securing with a toothpick.
Brown the rolls, then add the broth and simmer, covered, 1-1.25 hours until tender.
Serve with a spoonful of sour cream stirred into the sauce.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Season the beef slices with salt and pepper, place a spoonful of chopped onion on each, and roll up tightly, securing with a toothpick.
Dust the rolls lightly in flour and brown them on all sides.
Add the beef broth and bay leaf, cover, and simmer over low heat 1-1.25 hours until the beef is completely tender.
Remove the toothpicks and bay leaf, stir the sour cream into the sauce, and serve hot with kasza (buckwheat groats) or potatoes.
description: Zrazy, thin beef rolled around a filling and braised, a classic Polish home-cooking dish.

name: Polish Gulasz
steps: Brown the pork with the onion.
Add the tomatoes, bell pepper, and a splash of water, and simmer, covered, 1-1.25 hours until tender.
Serve with kasza or bread.
steps_detailed: Brown the pork with the onion and garlic over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the diced tomatoes, bell pepper, beef broth, oregano, and bay leaf. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and cook over low heat 1.25-1.5 hours, stirring occasionally, until the pork is completely tender and the sauce has thickened.
Remove the bay leaf and serve hot with kasza (buckwheat groats) or crusty bread - Poland's take on the Central European goulash tradition.
description: Gulasz, Poland's take on Hungarian goulash, with its own regional character.

name: Polish Oscypek-Style Grilled Cheese

ingredients.note: oz, sliced, as a smoked mountain cheese stand-in

steps: Grill the cheese slices in the butter until golden and slightly melted on the outside, 2 minutes per side.
Serve with a spoonful of the preserves on the side.

ingredients_full.note: oz, sliced, as a smoked mountain cheese stand-in

steps_detailed: Slice the cheese into thick rounds.
Grill in the butter over medium heat, 2-3 minutes per side, until golden and slightly softened but still holding its shape.
Serve warm with a spoonful of cranberry sauce, echoing the traditional pairing of smoked oscypek cheese with cranberry preserves in the Tatra mountains.
description: Oscypek, a smoked sheep's milk cheese from the Tatra mountains, traditionally grilled and topped with cranberry sauce.

name: Polish Kielbasa Bites
steps: Fry the kielbasa rounds with the onion until browned and lightly caramelized, about 6-8 minutes.
Serve warm with mustard.
steps_detailed: Slice the kielbasa into thick rounds.
Fry in the oil with the onion over medium heat, 6-8 minutes, until the kielbasa is browned and the onion is soft and golden.
Serve warm with mustard for dipping - a simple, popular Polish party snack.
description: Kiełbasa, Poland's most famous sausage, found in countless regional varieties.

name: Polish Faworki
steps: Mix the flour, egg, a pinch of salt, and a little water into a firm dough.
Roll thin, cut into strips, tie each into a loose knot, and fry until golden and crisp.
Dust with powdered or granulated sugar.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, egg, vanilla, a pinch of salt, and just enough water to form a firm, smooth dough. Rest 15 minutes.
Roll the dough very thin and cut into strips about 1 inch wide and 5 inches long, cutting a slit in the center of each and pulling one end through to form a loose knot (bow) shape.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the bows 1-2 minutes per side until puffed, golden, and crisp.
Drain briefly and dust generously with sugar while still warm - a beloved Polish carnival-season treat.
description: Faworki, thin fried pastry dusted with powdered sugar, a beloved treat especially for Fat Thursday.

name: Polish Krokiety
steps: Mix the shredded chicken with the cottage cheese for the filling.
Spoon onto the tortilla, roll up tightly, and fry until golden on all sides.
Slice and serve hot.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion until soft, then mix with the shredded chicken and cottage cheese for the filling.
Dip the roll in flour, then beaten egg, to help it hold together and crisp up.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the roll, turning to brown all sides, 5-6 minutes until golden and crisp.
Slice and serve hot - a popular Polish croquette, often made with leftover meat.
description: Spoon the filling onto a thin savory crepe or pancake (traditionally a simple flour-and-egg crepe), and roll up tightly into a cylinder.

name: Polish Szarlotka

ingredients.ingredient: Apples, raw, gala, with skin (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Cream the butter and sugar, then work in the flour to form a soft dough; press two-thirds into a small dish.
Top with the sliced apple.
Crumble the remaining dough over the top and bake at 350F for 35-40 minutes until golden.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Apples, raw, gala, with skin (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Cream the butter and sugar until light, then beat in the egg.
Sift together the flour and baking powder and work into the butter mixture until a soft, crumbly dough forms. Press about two-thirds into a small buttered baking dish.
Toss the sliced apples with the cinnamon and arrange over the base.
Crumble the remaining dough evenly over the top.
Bake at 350F (175C) for 35-45 minutes until the top is golden and the apples are tender - Poland's classic apple cake.
description: Szarlotka, Polish apple cake, one of the country's most beloved desserts.

name: Polish Paczki
steps: Mix the flour, egg, sugar, and a little warm milk into a soft dough. Rest 20 minutes.
Shape into small rounds, fill the center with a bit of the preserves, and seal.
Fry until puffed and golden, then dust with sugar.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, baking powder, egg, sugar, and warm milk into a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead briefly, cover, and rest 25-30 minutes until slightly puffed.
Divide into small balls, flatten each, place a spoonful of preserves in the center, and fold the dough around it, pinching firmly to seal.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the paczki 2-3 minutes per side until deeply golden and puffed, and cooked through.
Drain briefly and dust generously with sugar while still warm - Poland's beloved jam-filled doughnut, especially popular on Fat Thursday before Lent.
description: Pączki, filled doughnuts, are made in enormous quantities for Fat Thursday celebrations.

name: Polish Sernik
steps: Blend the cottage cheese until smooth.
Whisk in the eggs, sugar, and flour until well combined.
Pour into a small buttered dish and bake at 325F for 40-45 minutes until just set.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Raisins, dark, seedless (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Whisk in the eggs, sugar, flour, and vanilla until well combined and smooth.
Fold in the raisins.
Pour into a small buttered baking dish and bake at 325F (165C) for 40-50 minutes until just set with a slight wobble in the center.
Let cool completely, then chill at least 2 hours before slicing - Polish cheesecake is denser and less sweet than the American version, with a distinct farmer's cheese tang.
description: Blend the cottage cheese in a food processor until completely smooth and creamy (traditionally made with twaróg farmer's cheese, pressed and drained).

name: Polish Koperkowy (Dill Sauce)
steps: Whisk the sour cream, dill, and broth together until smooth.
Warm gently and serve over boiled potatoes or fish.
steps_detailed: Whisk the sour cream with the chicken broth until smooth, then stir in the chopped dill and garlic.
Warm gently over low heat, being careful not to let it boil hard (sour cream can separate).
Serve over boiled new potatoes, fish, or vegetables - a classic Polish dill and sour cream sauce.
description: Koperkowy, a dill sauce, dill being one of Poland's most-used herbs.

name: Polish Chrzanowy (Horseradish Sauce)
steps: Whisk the sour cream, vinegar, and sugar together.
Season with salt and serve alongside roast meats or boiled eggs.
steps_detailed: Whisk the sour cream, vinegar, sugar, and black pepper together until smooth.
Season with salt to taste and chill briefly before serving.
Traditionally made with fresh grated horseradish stirred into cream - this milder version pairs well with roast meats, ham, or boiled eggs.
description: Chrzanowy, a horseradish sauce, a classic Polish condiment especially for meat and eggs.
